skill.name: architecture-decision-record
skill.description: Create Architecture Decision Records using Nygard-style templates, examples, and review guidance for significant technical decisions.
skill.summary: Document architectural decisions with context, alternatives, consequences, and review criteria.
skill.author: AI Agent Hub

security_audit.summary: The static analyzer reported command execution, network, credential, and weak-crypto indicators, but review found documentation-only Markdown. The flagged locations are template paths, code spans, example URLs, and architecture terms, with no scripts, network calls, credential access, or prompt injection found.

low_findings.title: Static command execution findings are Markdown examples
low_findings.description: The external command detections are false positives. The flagged lines use Markdown code spans, fenced blocks, file paths, template examples, and technology names, not executable Ruby or shell code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed files are Markdown documentation and templates. The flagged backticks delimit inline code, examples, and fenced blocks rather than commands executed by the skill.

low_findings.title: Static network findings are reference links only
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URL detections are false positives. The URLs appear in ADR reference sections and YAML example metadata, with no code that fetches, posts, or exfiltrates data.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The links are documentation references and placeholder example metadata. No executable network client or instruction to transmit data was found in the reviewed context.

low_findings.title: Static sensitive-file and weak-crypto hits are terminology collisions
low_findings.description: The sensitive and weak-crypto detections are false positives caused by ordinary architecture text such as SAM/LocalStack, calculator.aws, ADR status fields, and database or API terms. No credential file access, cryptographic implementation, or malicious C2 behavior was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The surrounding context is ADR documentation language, not security-sensitive code. The scanner matched substrings and common platform names without a supporting execution or access path.

content.value_statement: Teams lose the reasons behind important technical choices, which causes repeated debates and inconsistent implementation. This skill creates structured ADRs with context, decisions, consequences, alternatives, and review guidance.
actual_capabilities: Drafts ADRs using context, decision, consequences, alternatives, and references sections.
Provides complete examples for microservices, database selection, and API versioning decisions.
Supplies YAML frontmatter fields for ADR metadata and lifecycle tracking.
Guides review with checklists for quality, stakeholder approval, and rejection criteria.
Helps compare alternatives with clear pros, cons, and rejection rationale.
Tracks ADR status values such as proposed, accepted, superseded, deprecated, and rejected.
limitations: Does not execute commands, inspect repositories, or create files automatically.
Does not replace architecture review by qualified technical stakeholders.
Does not validate cost, performance, or compliance claims against live systems.
Does not provide diagram generation or integration with project management tools.

use_cases.title: Record major platform decisions
use_cases.description: Document why a team chose a database, API style, deployment model, or service boundary.

use_cases.title: Preserve decision history
use_cases.description: Give new team members a clear record of past decisions, alternatives, ownership, and trade-offs.

use_cases.title: Prepare decisions for review
use_cases.description: Use checklists to confirm an ADR has context, consequences, realistic alternatives, and stakeholder approval.

prompt_templates.title: Draft a basic ADR
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner prompt for a single decision
prompt_templates.prompt: Create an ADR for choosing PostgreSQL as our primary database. Include context, decision, consequences, alternatives, and references.

prompt_templates.title: Compare architecture options
prompt_templates.prompt: Write an ADR comparing a modular monolith, microservices, and serverless architecture. Recommend one option and explain rejected alternatives.

prompt_templates.title: Review an existing ADR
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this ADR for missing context, unclear decision language, weak alternatives, incomplete consequences, and stakeholder approval gaps.

prompt_templates.title: Create an ADR program
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced prompt for team process design
prompt_templates.prompt: Design an ADR process for our engineering organization. Include status lifecycle, numbering, storage location, review roles, and superseding rules.

output_examples.input: Create an ADR for adopting URL path API versioning.
output_examples.output: A structured ADR with accepted status, API versioning context, a clear URL path decision, positive and negative consequences, rejected alternatives, and references.

output_examples.input: Review this ADR before architecture council approval.
output_examples.output: A review summary that flags missing requirements, vague ownership, weak alternatives, unbalanced consequences, and approval items to resolve.

output_examples.input: Document why we rejected serverless for order processing.
output_examples.output: An ADR alternative section explaining cold start latency, vendor lock-in, debugging complexity, team readiness, and why another approach was selected.

best_practices: Write the decision in clear present-tense language with named owners and expected timing.
Include at least two realistic alternatives with balanced pros, cons, and rejection reasons.
Keep accepted ADRs stable and create a new ADR when a decision is superseded.
anti_patterns: Writing implementation instructions instead of documenting the architectural decision and rationale.
Listing only benefits while hiding operational, cost, security, or maintenance trade-offs.
Using weak alternatives that were never seriously considered by the team.

faq.question: What is an Architecture Decision Record?
faq.answer: An ADR is a short document that records an important technical decision, its context, alternatives, and consequences.

faq.question: When should I create an ADR?
faq.answer: Create one when a decision affects architecture, team workflow, cost, security, scalability, or future development direction.

faq.question: Does this skill write implementation code?
faq.answer: No. It produces documentation guidance, templates, examples, and review checklists for architecture decisions.

faq.question: Can this skill help review an ADR?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes checklist guidance for clarity, alternatives, consequences, stakeholder approval, and rejection criteria.

faq.question: Which ADR format does it use?
faq.answer: It follows a Nygard-style structure with context, decision, consequences, alternatives, and references.

faq.question: How should ADRs be stored?
faq.answer: Store them with the project documentation, often in docs/adr or architecture/decisions, using sequential numbering.
